content:
More panels did not reduce mean time to understanding.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

We had forty-seven Grafana dashboards for the payments service. Forty-seven. One for latency. One for latency but grouped by region. One for latency but only on Tuesdays if someone remembered to toggle the variable.
When checkout failed at 2:13 A.M., the on-call engineer opened Grafana, stared at graphs, opened Slack, asked “which dashboard,” got three links, opened all three, and still could not answer the question finance was already asking in the war room channel: Are we failing to capture money, or failing to record captures we already made?
Mean time to acknowledgment was four minutes. Mean time to understanding was forty-one.
More panels did not reduce mean time to understanding. They increased mean time to scrolling.
Observability theater is when instrumentation exists, dashboards multiply, and nobody can translate a red line into a decision. The show looks professional. The audience is still confused.
The Inventory Nobody Wanted
After the incident we audited dashboards like expired milk.
→ Twelve dashboards duplicated the same RED metrics with different chart colors → Eight dashboards had no linked runbook or owner → Nineteen panels queried metrics that had not emitted in ninety days → Six dashboards were created by people who left; titles included their names → Three “SLO dashboards” used thresholds nobody could explain → One dashboard was literally named “DO NOT DELETE” with no other documentation
Total unique questions the forty-seven dashboards could answer under pressure: about four. Poorly.
We did not have an observability problem in the sense of missing data. We had a signal-to-noise problem dressed as maturity.
Flat truth: leadership loved dashboard count as a proxy for seriousness. Count is easy. Comprehension is not.
What Understanding Requires That Graphs Do Not Provide
Dashboards show that something changed. Incidents require knowing what kind of broken you are.
Checkout failures split into at least four stories:
→ Vendor timeout — money might not have moved; reconcile before refunding → Vendor hard decline — user issue; support path → Internal ledger write failure after successful capture — money moved; customer will complain; finance must not double-refund → Client-side double submit — duplicate charges; idempotency failure
Four stories. One red latency spike. Without structured logs and a decision tree, on-call picks a story by vibe.
What actually shortened mean time to understanding:
→ One primary dashboard per service — linked from catalog, owned, versioned → Golden signals plus business counters — captures attempted, captures succeeded, ledger writes succeeded, mismatch rate between them → Log queries saved by name — “checkout-failed-after-vendor-success” not “Explore tab archaeology” → Runbook first line names the fork — if metric A up and metric B flat, go to section 3 → Alert text includes hypothesis — “possible ledger drift after vendor success” not “p99 high”
We deleted thirty-one dashboards. Incidents got quieter. Nobody missed the Tuesday latency board.
Alerts Are Part of the Show
Dashboards are not the only theater. Alerts perform too.
We had two hundred alert rules. Twelve fired in the last quarter. Of those, nine were ignored because historical false positive rate was ninety percent.
Alert fatigue is observability theater for machines — lots of shouting, no trust.
Cleanup rules:
→ Every alert maps to a runbook section or it is deleted → Every alert has an owner who acked it in a drill within the last ninety days → Flapping alerts get fixed or removed — no “muted indefinitely” → Page only when a human must act soon; everything else is ticket or daily digest → Alert message states user impact in plain language
After cleanup: forty-one rules. More pages. Better pages. Mean time to ignore dropped to zero because we stopped crying wolf.
Dry fact: the cheapest observability win we had was rewriting alert text. Same threshold. Different comprehension.
The Three Questions On-Call Must Answer
We printed this on the inside of the runbook cover. Not metaphorically. Literally laminated.
→ Is money moving incorrectly or not moving at all? → Is the blast radius growing or stable? → What is the safe mitigation — rollback, disable feature, scale, or wait?
If your observability stack cannot help answer those in ten minutes, you have charts, not observability.
Training changed too. New on-call engineers did not get a Grafana tour. They got a simulated incident with broken checkout and a timer. Pass condition: correct fork chosen with evidence cited from metrics and logs, not gut.
Three engineers failed the first simulation. They passed the second. Dashboard count unchanged.
Building for Answers, Not Panels
Practical build order for teams drowning in graphs:
→ Define the top five questions incidents ask for your service → Ensure one metric or log-derived counter exists per question — name them in plain English → Build one dashboard that answers all five with links to saved queries → Write runbook forks that reference those names exactly → Delete everything not linked from the runbook or catalog → Review quarterly: did any incident spend more than ten minutes finding the right view?
Observability is not a museum for metrics. It is a navigation system for stress.
We still have graphs. We have fewer. They mean something. On-call sleeps more. Finance gets answers before the third “any update?” message.
Forty-seven dashboards looked like maturity. Four answerable questions were maturity. Everything else was theater with excellent lighting.
The Dashboard Funeral We Actually Held
Deleting thirty-one dashboards risked hurt feelings. We scheduled a thirty-minute “dashboard funeral” — name each removed board, who created it, why it existed, why it no longer earned a slot.
Some creators were still employed. Some were not. A few dashboards were genuinely useful in 2021 and wrong after a rewrite. Acknowledging history made deletion feel like curation, not vandalism.
One engineer said: “I kept that board because I was afraid someone would ask for it in an audit.” Nobody had asked in three years. Fear is a common dashboard storage strategy.
After the funeral, we kept a redirect file: old dashboard URL maps to primary board section. Bookmarks stopped 404ing. Small kindness. Big reduction in Slack “where did X go.”
Metrics Naming as Documentation
Flat rule that stuck: if on-call cannot guess the metric name under stress, the name is wrong.
Renames we shipped:
→ checkout_capture_success_total instead of payment_ok → ledger_write_after_vendor_mismatch instead of sync_error → duplicate_capture_detected instead of anomaly_flag_7
Grafana autocompletes became training wheels. Saved queries stopped being oral tradition.
On-call handoff improved when outgoing primary recorded which saved query they used for the last incident. Incoming primary stopped reinventing Explore tabs at 2 A.M.
Flat metric: mean time to understanding dropped from forty-one minutes to nineteen over two quarters. Dashboard count dropped seventy percent. Correlation is not proof. It matched when we asked on-call what changed. Answer: fewer graphs, better names, runbook forks.
Logs Without Structure Are Graphs in Disguise
We had JSON logs. We also had string concatenation inside messages, unparsed error codes, and request IDs buried in prose. Searching was theater too.
Structured fields we enforced:
→ checkout_session_id on every payment log line → vendor_outcome enum, not free text → failure_class matching runbook fork names exactly
Saved queries became one click. Mean time to understanding dropped again. Dry work. High leverage.
Vendor dashboards are a special case of theater — latency graphs without error code breakdown tell you the stage is on fire without naming which actor dropped the line.
We require vendor error class in the same view as latency for any external dependency on the money path. One panel. Two axes of truth.
Observability maturity is not panel count. It is whether a tired human can answer a finance question before the third Slack ping.
Delete one dashboard this week that nobody opened in ninety days. If someone complains, ask them which runbook fork it supports. Silence means funeral.
When you want the production patterns for metrics, logs, alerts, and the system design judgment that turns instrumentation into answers — The Production Engineering Library: The Complete Collection for Production Systems, System Design & Backend Engineering is what I read before adding another panel.
Practice incident navigation — which metric to open first, which fork fits the failure — on Froquiz with scenario drills across SQL, Docker, Git, AWS, Python, Java, JavaScript, React, microservices, and Senior Dev Challenge problems shaped like 2 A.M. checkout pages, not chart trivia.
→ Froquiz
